texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In petrochemical refineries C4 paraffins (i.e. n-butane and isobutane) and C4 olefins (i.e. cis -2-butene, trans 2-butene, 1-butene, isobutylene and 1,3-butadiene) are generated in large scale. The conversion of crude oil in useable products involves several processing stages. A large volume of heavy fraction obtained from the distillation unit is processed in a fluid catalytic cracking (FCC) unit while naptha and gas oils are processed in a steam cracking (SC) unit. The C4 paraffins and olefins are generated in both of these process units. Some of these hydrocarbons are attractive chemical feedstocks for other industries. The 1,3-butadiene content of the C4 hydrocarbon stream produced by SC varies between 28 to 49 wt% while that produced by FCC is 0.2 wt%. 1,3-butadiene is a valuable  chemical  as  it  is  used  as  a  monomer  for  the  production  of elastomers which has huge demand in automobile industries for tires. 1butene is used as a comonomer for low density polyethylene. The C4 hydrocarbon stream produced by FCC and SC contains around 14 -20 wt %  1-butene.  The  isobutylene  content  of  the  C4  hydrocarbon  stream produced by SC and FCC varies between 22 and 32 wt%. Isobutylene is used  as  chemical  feed  stock  for  production  of  polyisobutylene  and chemical intermediates such as-methacrylic acid and its derivatives. The C4  olefins  are  also  converted  in  other  useful  products  directly  in petrochemical  refineries.  Acid  catalyzed  selective  etherification  by methanol  and  ethanol  is  used  to  convert  the  isobutylene  of  the  C4

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Separation of C4 paraffins and C4 olefins from each other is an underdeveloped sector of membrane gas separation. Permeabilitieis, diffusivities and solubilities of C4 paraffins (i.e. n-butane and isobutane) and C4 olefins (i. e. cis -2-butene, trans 2-butene, 1-butene, isobutylene and 1,3-butadiene) in three commercial polymers- PEBAX 2533, PolyActive1500PEGT77PBT23 and PolyActive4000PEGT77PBT23 are reported in this study for the first time. The aim of this study is to thoroughly understand the single gas permeation mechanism of the C4 paraffins and C4 olefins through the multiblock copolymers. The cohesive energy density of the polyether segments plays a vital role to determine the permeabilities through the polymer. The selectivity of the olefin/paraffin gas pairs are largely dependent on the content of the ether oxygen of the polyether segments. But the selectivity of the paraffin gas pair and the olefin isomer gas pairs are not influenced by the ether oxygen content of the polyether segments of the multiblock copolymers.

The aim of this study is to thoroughly understand the single gas permeation mechanism of the C4 paraffins and C4 olefins through the multiblock copolymers. The cohesive energy density of the polyether segments plays a vital role to determine the permeabilities through the polymer. The selectivity of the olefin/paraffin gas pairs are largely dependent on the content of the ether oxygen of the polyether segments. But the selectivity of the paraffin gas pair and the olefin isomer gas pairs are not influenced by the ether oxygen content of the polyether segments of the multiblock copolymers.

texts.orig: Due to the potential of valorization of the C4 olefins the separation of C4 hydrocarbons  has  recently  received  considerable  attention  by  researchers [2]. While distillation is conventionally used for hydrocarbon separation, alternative energy efficient separation processes are under investigation. Significant progress has been made for separation of C4 hydrocarbons by adsorption using microporous materials such as zeolites [3 -5], metal organic frameworks [6,7] etc. Membrane separation is an attractive alternative for hydrocarbon separation compared to the thermally  driven  separation  owing  to  the  low  energy  requirement. Economical and technical feasibility of implementing hybrid processes to combine distillation and membrane separation for C4 hydrocarbon separations  are  under  investigation  [8,9].  Membrane  material  development is a crucial part of the success of implementation of membrane separation process for C4 hydrocarbon separation. In this regard inorganic [10 -12], polymeric [13] and mixed matrix [14] membrane materials  have  been  reported.  Compared  to  the  separation  of  C1 -C3 hydrocarbons  the  polymeric  membranes  have  been  significantly  less

texts.orig: explored for the separation of C4 hydrocarbons [15 -17]. Gas permeation through polymeric membranes occur via solution-diffusion model. The gas molecules are first adsorbed at the feed side of the membrane followed by diffusion through the thickness of the membrane. Therefore, polymeric  membranes  can  efficiently  separate  the  gases  which  has substantially different solubility or diffusivity in the polymeric membranes. In other words, to separate two gases a polymeric membrane should have sufficient diffusion selectivity or solubility selectivity for one gas over the other. The diffusion selectivity originates from the size difference of the gas molecules while the solubility selectivity originates from the difference in condensability of the gas molecules (discussed in detail in section 3). The gas separation mechanism also depends on the physical properties of the polymeric membranes. In general, the selectivity of the rubbery polymeric membranes are largely dominated by solubility  selectivity  while  that  of  glassy  polymeric  membranes  are dominated by diffusion selectivity. Separation of gaseous hydrocarbons by taking advantage of their size difference is a conventional task of glassy polymer membranes. Polyimides have been explored for separation  of  1,3-butadiene/n-butane [18 -20] and n-butane/isobutane [14, 21]. Both diffusion selectivity and solubility selectivity contributes to the  overall  selectivity  of  1,3-butadiene/n-butane  in  polyimide  membranes. The n-butane/isobutane separation by polyimide membrane is largely dictated by diffusion selectivity. In contrast to the conventional polymers,  polyacetylenes  have  received  lot  of  attention  for  faster permeation of n-butane compared to smaller alkanes (i.e. for solubility selectivity) inspite of being glassy polymers [22 -25]. However, polyacetylenes have not been explored for the separation of C4 paraffins and C4 olefins from each other. For the light gases (e.g. H2, N2, O2, CH4 etc) it is well established that the polymeric membranes are confronted with a trade-off between permeability and selectivity [26 -29]. The polymeric membranes having high selectivity are less permeable and vice versa. In a log-log plot of selectivity versus permeability of the more permeable gas, L.M Robeson [30,31] related this trade-off to a linear upper bound (often  referred  to  as  Robeson ' s  upper  bound  curve).  Later,  Benny

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Freeman  derived  a  theoretical  basis  of  the  upper  bound  [32].  The theoretical  analysis  revealed  designing  polymeric  membranes  having high solubility selectivity could be an effective way to overcome the upper  bound.  For  light  gases,  researchers  frequently  use  the  upper bound to benchmark the sepration performance of new membrane materials. However, due to lack of investigation such upper bound remains to be established for most of the C4 hydrocarbon pairs. In 2020 for the first time Iyer et al. [15] reported an upper bound for 1,3-butadiene/n-butane gas pair from literature survey. In short, development of polymeric membrane materials for separation of C4 paraffins and C4 olefins from each other remains an underdeveloped sector.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lin and Freeman [33] reported that the permeability of C2H4 > C2H6 and  C3H6 > C3H8  through  poly(ethyele  oxide)  (PEO)  homopolymer having 71% crystallinity. The olefins have specific interaction with PEO [33,34]. The crystallites of a semicrystalline polymer acts as a barrier for gas permeation. Therefore, either crosslinked polyethers [35] or multiblock copolymers containing polyether segments have become popular alternatives as gas separation membrane materials. A large number of multiblock copolymers containing alterating series of polyether block and a glassy or semicrystalline block (e.g. polyimide [36], polyurethane [37], polyamide [38 -41], polyester [42 -44], etc) have been used for gas separation. In these multiblock copolymers the polyether segments are covalently  attached  with  other  blocks  in  two  ends.  As  a  result  the conformational freedom to pack in well ordered crystals reduce significantly. This class of polymers have become an ideal choice for sepration of CO2 from light gases. But the potential of such multiblock copolymers to separate C4 paraffins and C4 olefins remains to be explored. This work is focused on investigating the permeation of the C4 paraffins and C4 olefins through three commercial multiblock copolymers and assessing their potential as membrane materials. The three commercial polymers are PEBAX 2533, PolyActive1500PEGT77PBT23 and PolyActive4000PEGT77PBT23.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: PEBAX 2533 was purchased from ARKEMA. PolyActive1500PEGT77PBT23 and PolyActive4000PEGT77PBT23 were purchased  from  PolyVation.  Dichloromethane  (purity  99.0%)  and  nbutanol (purity 99.5%) were purchased from Merck KGaA and Scharlau Chemie S.A. respectively. The chemicals were used as received without any further purification.

Dichloromethane  (purity  99.0%)  and  nbutanol (purity 99.5%) were purchased from Merck KGaA and Scharlau Chemie S.A. respectively. The chemicals were used as received without any further purification.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In  this  work  solution  casting  was  used  to  prepare  dense  films  of PEBAX 2533, PolyActive1500PEGT77PBT23 and PolyActive4000PEGT77PBT23.  Dichloromethane  was  used  as  solvent  to prepare solutions of PolyActive1500PEGT77PBT23 and PolyActive4000PEGT77PBT23. The solution of PEBAX2533 was prepared at 70 ◦ C using n-butanol as solvent. The polymer solutions were poured in Teflon molds. The solution of P2533 was evaporated at 40 ◦ C while the solutions of PolyActive1500PEGT77PBT23 and PolyActive4000PEGT77PBT23 were evaporated at room temperature. The films were dried under vacuum overnight at 30 ◦ C. A Deltascope FMP10 digital  micrometer produced by HELMUT FISCHER GMBH, Germany was used to determine the thickness of the obtained films. The thickness was  determined  at  fifty  different  points  of  the  prepared  films.  The average thickness and standard deviation of the thickness across the films  were  calculated.  The  average  thickness  of  PEBAX  2533,  PolyActive1500PEGT77PBT23 and PolyActive4000PEGT77PBT23 are 203.5 ± 7.6 μ m, 197.0 ± 7.3 μ m and 198.1 ± 2 μ m.

texts.orig: The melting transitions of PEBAX 2533, PolyActive1500PEGT77PBT23 and PolyActive1500PEGT77PBT23 was investigated in the temperature range from 100 ◦ C to 250 ◦ C using differential  scanning  calorimetry  (DSC).  The  DSC  thermograms  were recorded using a DSC 1 (Star system) from Mettler Toledo. A nitrogen purge gas stream (60 mL/min) and a scan rate of 10 K/min was used for all DSC runs. The samples were initially heated up to 100 ◦ C to remove the  residual  solvent,  then  the  sample  was  cooled  down  to 100 ◦ C

texts.orig: Where A is the membrane area, Vp is the permeate volume, pf is the feed pressure  considered  constant  in  the  time  range Δ t,  pp1 and  pp2 are permeate pressures at times 1 and 2, Δ t is the time difference between two points (1 and 2) on the pressure curve, l is the membrane thickness, R is the gas constant and θ is the time lag [34,41]. The permeate pressure was recorded until the curve attains 400 points (at regular time interval) in the steady state i.e. linear zone. The feed pressure of the gases was 0.5 bar. Each gas was measured three times to ensure the reproducibility of the  experiments.  The  average  values  are  standard  deviations  were calculated.

texts.orig: In  the  following  text  PEBAX  2533,  PolyActive1500PEGT77PBT23 and  PolyActive4000PEGT77PBT23  is  denoted  as  P2533,  P1500  and P4000 respectively. The chemical structure of the polymers are depicted in Fig. 1. P2533 is composed of 20 wt% polyamide 12 and 80 wt% PTMO [34,38,39,45]. P1500 and P4000 are composed of 23% poly(butylene terephthalate)  and  77  wt%  poly(ethylene  glycol)terephthalate.  The length of polyethylene oxide (PEO) segments of P1500 and P4000 are 1500 and 4000 g/mol respectively [34,42,46]. The melting transitions of the multiblock copolymers P2533, P1500 and P4000 are plotted in the DSC thermograms of Fig. 1.

The chemical structure of the polymers are depicted in Fig. 1. P2533 is composed of 20 wt% polyamide 12 and 80 wt% PTMO [34,38,39,45]. P1500 and P4000 are composed of 23% poly(butylene terephthalate)  and  77  wt%  poly(ethylene  glycol)terephthalate.  The length of polyethylene oxide (PEO) segments of P1500 and P4000 are 1500 and 4000 g/mol respectively [34,42,46]. The melting transitions of the multiblock copolymers P2533, P1500 and P4000 are plotted in the DSC thermograms of Fig. 1.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Two distinct melting endotherm are observed for P2533 which corresponds to the poly(tetramethylene oxide) and polyamide 12 blocks respectively  [34,45].  Similarly,  the  microphase  separated  P1500  and P4000 show two melting endotherms for the poly(ethylene oxide) (PEO) and  poly(butylene  terephthalate)  (PBT)  respectively  [34,46].  The meting transitions of PBT in both P1500 and P4000 are rather broad having onset temperatures above 100 ◦ C. Owing to the different length of the PEO segments the onset and endset of the melting endotherms of the PEO block of P1500 and P4000 are significantly different. In this study, the gas permeation properties are investigated at 33 ◦ C. For the PTMO block of P2533 a broad melting endotherm is observed between 14 and 32 ◦ C (Fig. 1). The onset and endset temperature of the melting endotherm of the PEO block of P1500 is 18 ◦ C and 32 ◦ C respectively (Fig. 1). The melting of the PEO block of P4000 starts at 40 ◦ C and ends at 49 ◦ C (Fig. 1). Hence, at 33 ◦ C the polyether blocks of P2533 and P1500 are completely amorphous while the polyether blocks of P4000 are in a semicrystalline state. The permeability, diffusitivity and solubility  of  the  gases are studied at 33 ◦ C in order to shed light on gas permeation  mechanism  through  amorphous  PTMO  of  P2533,  amorphous PEO of P1500 and semicrystalline PEO of P4000. The content of polyether  blocks  of  the  three  polymer  are  rather  similar  and  gas permeation  in  these  polymers  occurs  mostly  through  the  polyether phase. Thus, a comparison of the gas permeation behavior of P1500 and P2533 at 33 ◦ C reveals the influence of the difference of cohesive energy density of amorphous PEO and amorphous PTMO on gas permeation mechanism. On the other hand, the influence of the presence of PEO crystallites on gas permeation mechanism are revealed by a comparison of the permeability, diffusitivity and solubility of the gases in P1500 and P4000 at 33 ◦ C. A detail discussion in this regard is provided in section 3.2.

As up to five phases can coexist in the multiblock copolymers studied in this work, using the two phase model for quantitative correlation is likely to be an over simplification of the actual membrane morphology. But the model can be used to make qualitative comparison among the multiblock copolymers. Both P4000 and P1500 are composed of 77 wt% poly(ethylene glycol)terephthalate while the remaining  23  wt%  is  PBT.  The  content  of  PBT  crystalline  phase  is approximately 6 wt% with respect to the total weight of the polymers. At 33 ◦ C the PBT crystals remain intact as the melting point is above 100 ◦ C. P1500 has a completely amorphous PEO phase at 33 ◦ C. The crystallinity of the PEO block of P4000 is 57% [46]. As the onset on the meting of PEO block of P4000 is 40 ◦ C it is clear that only 43% of the PEO exists in an  amorphous  during  gas  permeation  measurements  i.e.  at  33 ◦ C.

texts.orig: Therefore, the total amorphous phase content (i.e. φ a of equation (4)) of P1500 is substantially higher than P4000. As a result, in accordance with equation  (4)  all  hydrocarbons  have  significantly  higher  permeability through P1500 compared to P4000 (Fig. 2). Metz et al. [43] investigated gas permeation of a series of PolyActive multiblock copolymers with 25 -60 wt% PBT content above the melting point of PEO block. The permeabilities  gradually  decreased  with  the  increase  of  PBT  content which proved gas permeation mostly occur through the amorphous PEO phase and the contribution of amorphous PBT phase can be neglected. At 33 ◦ C,  compared  to  the  content  of  amorphous  polyether  blocks,  the content  of  amorphous  poly(butylene  terephthalate)  and  amorphous polyamide 12 are substantially low in P1500 and P2533 respectively. The gas permeation through P1500 and P2533 is governed the by polyether phase  while that  through  amorphous  poly(butylene  terephthalate)  and  amorphous  polyamide  12  can  be  neglected.  The permeabilities of the C4 hydrocarbons through P2533 are several time higher compared to those of P1500 although they have similar polyether block  content.  It  orginates  from  the  difference  in  cohesive  energy density between the PTMO segments and the PEO segments of P2533 and P1500 respectively [34]. At a fixed temperature the cohesive energy density  of  the  polymer  segments  can  be  used  as  a  measure  of  their intermolecular interaction energy. As the content of polar ether oxygen of PTMO is lower than PEO, the attractive forces between the PTMO segments are also lower i.e. the cohesive energy of PTMO is lower than that of PEO [51,52]. Therefore, the PTMO blocks of P2533 offers lower resistance against the permeation of the hydrocarbons compared to the PEO blocks of P1500. The role of cohesive energy density on kinetic and thermodynamic factor of permeabilities can be examined by comparing the diffusivities (Fig. 3) and solubilities (Fig. 4) of the hydrocarbons in P2533 and P1500 respectively.

Hence, the lower  cohesive  energy  density  of  the  PTMO  segments  facilitates  the faster diffusion of the hydrocarbons through P2533 compared to P1500. Nevertheless, as several other factors are involved, a higher cohesive energy  does  not  always  lead  to  lower  fractional  free  volume  of  the polymer [54]. As the C4 hydrocarbons are non-spherical molecules it is rather difficult to choose an ideal scaling parameter to properly represent the size difference of the gas molecules. In Fig. 3 the diffusivities of the hydrocarbons are plotted against their critical volume (the critical volumes  values  reported  by  Li  et  al.  [55]  are  used).  In  general,  the critical volume is considered to be a more ideal scaling parameter to represent the size of the non-spherical gas molecules compared to other scaling parameters e.g. kinetic diameter [53,56,57]. Fig. 3 demonstrates that the critical volumes of the penetrating gases is highly relevant to determine  the  diffusivities  of  the  C4  hydrocarbons  through  P2533, P1500 and P4000. However, the diffusivities of all the C4 hydrocarbon do  not  change  depending  only  on  their  critical  volumes.  There  are additional factors involved to dictate the diffusion of the hydrocarbons.

texts.orig: incident can also contribute to the hindered diffusion due to τ . From an energy consideration, β is also influenced by the diffusing gas i.e. the amorphous region next to crystallites where the segmental mobility is reduced can allow faster diffusion of a smaller gas [48 -50]. 1,3-butadiene is the smallest gas studied here and the diffusivity of 1,3-butadiene appears to be slightly less hindered compared to other gases. It implies some interlamellar gap of amorphous regions might be exist in P4000 which allow only the diffusive jumps of 1,3-butadiene. The other hydrocarbons do not make the diffusive jumps through these regions as the energy required to make the diffusive jump is larger due their slightly larger size compared to 1,3 butadiene. From Fig. 3 it is evident that, isobutylene and isobutane have additional resistance to diffuse through the polyether blocks of the multiblock copolymers than the other C4 hydrocarbons. As both P2533 and P1500 shows the same trend, this additional resistance does not originate from the cohesive energy density  of  the  polyether  blocks.  It  originates  from  the  nature  of  the  gas molecules. The 3d structures of all the C4 hydrocarbons are provided in Fig. 2. Among the C4 hydrocarbons only isobutane and isobutylene have the spatial arrangement of atoms where a central tertiary carbon atom is bonded with three other carbon atoms. It is obvious that this specific spatial  arrangement  of  atoms  provide  additional  hindrance  to  their diffusion  through the multiblock copolymers irrespective of cohesive energy density of the polyether blocks. The reason behind the faster diffusion of trans -2-butene in all three polymers compared to what is expected from its critical volume is not clear. Among the C4 olefins only 1,3-butadiene and trans -2-butene has no dipole moment [2]. Unlike 1, 3-butadiene the double bond of trans -2-butene is shielded by the -CH3 moieties in both ends. It is not possible to comment from this study whether  such  molecular  features  of trans -2-butene  facilitates  faster diffusion  through  the  PEO  and  PTMO  containing  multiblock  copolymers.  Instead,  this  study  raises  such  questions  for  future  investigations e.g. by computer simulation.

It  originates  from  the  nature  of  the  gas molecules. The 3d structures of all the C4 hydrocarbons are provided in Fig. 2. Among the C4 hydrocarbons only isobutane and isobutylene have the spatial arrangement of atoms where a central tertiary carbon atom is bonded with three other carbon atoms. It is obvious that this specific spatial  arrangement  of  atoms  provide  additional  hindrance  to  their diffusion  through the multiblock copolymers irrespective of cohesive energy density of the polyether blocks. The reason behind the faster diffusion of trans -2-butene in all three polymers compared to what is expected from its critical volume is not clear. Among the C4 olefins only 1,3-butadiene and trans -2-butene has no dipole moment [2]. Unlike 1, 3-butadiene the double bond of trans -2-butene is shielded by the -CH3 moieties in both ends. It is not possible to comment from this study whether  such  molecular  features  of trans -2-butene  facilitates  faster diffusion  through  the  PEO  and  PTMO  containing  multiblock  copolymers.  Instead,  this  study  raises  such  questions  for  future  investigations e.g. by computer simulation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There are two paraffins i.e. n-butane and isobutane among the C4 hydrocarbons. Fig. 5a shows the selectivity of n-butane over isobutane is above 2 in all 3 polymers. Both diffusion selectivity (Fig. 5b) and solubility selectivity (Fig. 5c) contributes to the n-butane/isobutane selectivity. There is no significant difference between the n-butane/isobutane selectivity of P2533 and P1500 (Fig. 5a). But P2533 has slightly higher diffusion selectivity (Fig. 5b) and lower solubility selectivity (Fig. 5c) compared to P1500. As already discussed in section 3.2, due to large size the  diffusivity  of  the  isobutane  is  lower  than  n-butane  in  all  three polymers. Due lower cohesive energy density of the soft segments, the diffusivity  of  both  n-butane  and  isobutane  through  P2533  is  higher compared to P1500. But Fig. 5b shows inspite of having the polyether segments having lower cohesive energy density P2533 exhibits better size selectivity of the C4 paraffin gas pairs compared to P1500. P4000 has slightly higher the n-butane/isobutane selectivity compared to that of P1500. From a careful comparison of the error bars of it is not possible to conclude whether the diffusion selectivity (Fig. 5b) or the solubility selectivity (Fig. 5c) has a larger contribution to the slightly higher nbutane/isobutane  selectivity  of  P4000.  The  permeability  of  n-butane and isobutane through P2533 is approximately 10 times higher than P1500 while 70 and 100 times higher than P4000 respectively. Thus among 3 polymer P2533 i.e. the polymer having poly(tetramethylene oxide) soft block is the ideal material for sepration of C4 paraffins.

The  critical  temperature  of  isobutane  is significantly lower than that of n-butane (Fig. 4). As a result the solubility selectivities of the olefin/isobutane gas pairs are always higher than  the  olefin/n-butane  gas  pairs  respectively  (Figs.  6 -10).  On  the other  hand  as  isobutane  has  larger  critical  volume  than  n-butane (Fig. 3), the diffusion selectivities of the olefin/isobutane gas pairs are also higher than the olefin/n-butane gas pairs. For  example -the selectivities  of isobutylene/isobutane, cis -2-butene/isobutane, trans -2-butene/isobutane and 1-butene/isobutane are 4.5, 10, 10.3 and 5.2 respectively. The selectivity of 1,3-butadiene/isobutane is 18.8. The excellent selectivity of  1,3-butadiene  over  the  paraffins  originate  from  a  combination  of diffusion  and  solubility  selectivity.  1,3-butadiene  being  the  smallest molecule among the C4-hydrocarbons has higher diffusion selectivity over paraffins compared to the other olefins. Moreover, as 1,3-butadiene is the only C4 olefin having two double bonds, it has higher solubility selectivity over paraffins compared to the other olefins. For all olefin paraffin gas pairs P1500 has significantly higher selectivity compared to P2533  owing  to  higher  solubility  selectivity.  For  every  C4  olefin/ isobutane  gas  pairs  P2533  has  slightly  higher  diffusion  selectivity compared to P1500. However, that is not the case for C4 olefin/n-butane gas pairs. The  overall  selectivity  of  all olefin/paraffin gas pairs in P2533 and P1500 is largely dominated by solubility selectivity. Therefore, it is evident that the PEO containing P1500 is a better choice for separation of C4 olefins from C4 paraffins

texts.orig: 4. Conclusion
texts.text: 4. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this study single gas permeation of C4 paraffins and C4 olefins are performed  to  thoroughly  investigate  their  permeation  mechanism through  three  commercial  multiblock  copolymers  having  polyether segments.  It  was  found  the  diffusivities  of  the  gases  through  these polymers does not depend only of the critical volume of the gases. The diffusivities of isobutane and isobutylene are lower and that of trans -2butene  are  higher  compared  to  what  is  expected  from  their  critical volumes. The selectivities in these polymers are largely dictated by the solubility selectivities of the gas pairs. The thorough investigation reveals the potential of using these polymers for sepration of C4 paraffins and C4 olefins from each other. Among the three polymers PEBAX 2533 has the highest permeability of all the studied hydrocarbons owing to the low cohesive energy density of the polyether segments. Moreover, the selectivities of paraffin isomer gas pair and the olefin isomer gas pairs of PEBAX 2533 is similar like two grades of PolyActives. Thus a multiblock copolymer containing poly(ethyelene oxide) segments do not have  any  advantage  over  a  polymer  containing  poly(tetramethylene oxide) segments for the separation of C4 paraffin isomers and C4 olefin isomers from each other. For these gas pairs the solubility selectivities are determined by the condensabilities of the gas molecules. The polymers containing poly(ethylene oxide) segments are ideal for separation of C4 olefins from C4 paraffins and 1,3-butadiens from other olefins. Along with the difference in condensabilities of the gas molecules the specific affinity of the olefins to the polar ether oxygens contribute to the solublity selectivities of the olefin/paraffin and 1,3-butadiene/olefin gas pairs. The highest selectivities were observed for 1,3-butadiene/isobutane  gas  pairs  (approximately  19)  in  both  grades  of  PolyActives
